meta.title: Demo essay: Coherence and form
meta.subtitle: Standard ensaYOnesa template
meta.author: OptimeFlow(s) · Example
meta.note: This is a demo essay generated by the internal ensaYOnesa template. You can safely write over it: all the content is fictional and intended only to test the format.
meta.summary: The aim of this demo essay is to show what the standard ensaYOnesa format looks like: cover, table of contents, chapters, subsections and justified text. You can modify titles, text and styles without touching the code.

s0.title: I. Starting point
s0.body:
This first demo chapter sums up the idea of a simple, readable essay. The cover, table of contents and body text are designed to work together, so you do not have to worry about layout.

You can use this chapter as a draft: change the titles, add your own paragraphs and see how the template keeps the standard format.

s1.title: I.1. Initial question
s1.body:
This is where the first real subsection of the chapter would go. A good beginning usually answers three questions: what do I want to say, who am I writing for, and what form do I need so it can be understood calmly.

In this example, the focus is on showing you the flow: cover → table of contents → chapter → subsections. From here on, everything you write will already be your own essay.

s2.title: I.2. Context and methodological note
s2.body:
This second sample subsection shows how the text is distributed when paragraphs extend over several lines. The line spacing and justification are designed for reading both on screen and on paper, without you having to adjust anything.

You can use this subsection to explain the context of your essay: where the idea comes from, which sources you have in mind, and what your text does not aim to do.

s0.title: II. Body of the essay
s0.body: This sample chapter represents the heart of the text: the place where you develop your argument, your scenes or your data. You can divide it into as many subsections as you need; here we show you two so you can see the basic behaviour.

s1.title: II.1. Main development
s1.body:
The first subsection of the body of the essay usually contains the strongest part of the argument: the one thing you want the reader to remember even if they only skim the text.

In this space you can combine longer paragraphs with shorter ones. The template takes care of preserving margins, typography and justification.

s2.title: II.2. Nuances and examples
s2.body:
Here you could add specific examples, small scenes, data or references. When paragraphs connect calmly, the reading experience feels like a continuous thread rather than a list of separate ideas.

You can delete this text and write directly over it: the chapter and subsection structure will keep working in exactly the same way.

s0.title: III. Closing and next steps
s0.body:
The last chapter of this example is intended for your conclusion: what you take from the process, what you offer the reader and which paths open up after the text.

A clear closing helps the essay remain not just a series of interesting ideas, but a form that can be revisited and shared.

s1.title: III.1. Synthesis
s1.body:
In the synthesis you can gather the main lines of the essay in a couple of paragraphs: what the starting question was, what you discovered and what remains open.

The idea is that someone who comes directly here can understand, in just a few lines, the skeleton of the text.

s2.title: III.2. Invitation to the reader
s2.body:
Finally, this space can serve as an invitation: what the person who has read the essay can do now, what small gesture they can try, or what conversation they could open.

A good conclusion does not close everything, but it does point to a possible next step.

citations.c1: Demo citation 1: short reference to test the footer.
citations.c2: Demo citation 2: a somewhat longer reference to force a line break in the footer and check margins.
citations.c3: Demo citation 3: another reference to see several citations stacked on the same page.
citations.c4: Demo citation 4: an example citation of medium length (used to test the final citations appendix).
citations.c6: Demo citation 6: test citation with a bit more length to check word-wrap.

privacy.title: Privacy policy
privacy.body:
ensaYOnesa works mainly in your browser and in your device's local storage. The essay content, preview and PDF export are processed locally in the app itself.

  • Local data: the app may store the project, settings, language, templates and some local resources in LocalStorage, SessionStorage, IndexedDB and browser caches to remember your preferences and work offline.
  • Voice dictation: dictation uses the browser's speech recognition API. Depending on the browser, device and its configuration, audio may be processed locally or sent to the recognition service of the browser or system provider. ensaYOnesa does not upload that audio or the contents of your files to OptimeFlow(s)'s own servers.
  • Access: this version can be used without signing in.
  • Cookies and tracking: we do not use tracking cookies or advertising analytics in the app.
  • Deletion: the “Clear” button resets the project saved by the app. To also remove caches and other site data, use your browser's option to clear site data.
  • License: the software is provided “as is”, without warranties.

Author: Andrés Calvo Espinosa.
Last updated: 2026-03-16.

coherence.title: Universal Coherence
coherence.body: May this work serve as gratitude and commitment: to think, feel and act in the same direction, without "ornaments"; to keep creating with ethics, joy and respect for everything that connects us.
